text: The surgeon begins a robotic bilateral inguinal hernia repair by creating a large peritoneal flap starting on the right-hand side and extending towards the midline, removing the umbilical ligaments and midline preperitoneal fat.

The surgeon continues dissecting through the fatty tissue towards the patient's left side, creating a peritoneal flap. The dissection proceeds medially towards Cooper's ligament, aiming to reach the pubic area.

The surgeon performs a lateral dissection after completing the medial dissection, revealing a direct inguinal hernia with a large amount of preperitoneal fat. The hernia is visible and tends to reduce easily.
The surgeon sweeps the cord structures off the peritoneum, preparing to parietalize these structures by separating them from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies and reduces a small lipoma to prevent it from interfering with the placement of the mesh during the hernia repair.
The surgeon proceeds to the left side, addressing a small direct hernia and reducing the preperitoneal fat.

The surgeon peels the peritoneum off the cord structures on the left side and reduces the obturator fat plug. For larger direct defects, the surgeon invaginates the defect to Cooper's ligament to decrease seroma formation and support mesh integration.
The surgeon places a twelve by sixteen mesh on the right side, followed by another twelve by sixteen mesh on the left side, covering the hernia defects.
The surgeon closes the flap by starting suturing at the midline, then proceeding to sew towards the patient's left side, and finally completing the closure on the right side.
